Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: Buying or replacing revenue-producing equipment, trade tools, shop machines, lifts, chippers, diagnostic systems, excavators, pavers, HVAC units, or specialty gear.
Primary Capital Pressure: Large asset purchase would drain operating cash.
Typical Trades: HVAC; excavation; concrete; paving; tree service; auto repair; septic; towing; well drilling; landscaping
What It Covers: New or used equipment, machinery, tools, shop equipment, revenue-producing assets.
Best Signals: Equipment photos; new service line; operator/technician hiring; backlog; posts about broken/outdated equipment; heavier jobs.
Not A Fit When: Need is purely payroll, marketing, or receivables; business is too new/thin; equipment is not tied to revenue.
Outreach Angle: Equipment makes money, but buying it outright can choke cash. Compare options that keep cash free while the gear goes to work.
Example CTA: Want me to show what equipment funding could look like?
Compliance Notes: Do not imply approval, rate, term, or asset eligibility without lender review.

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: Adding, replacing, or repairing trucks, vans, trailers, wreckers, service vehicles, or delivery units.
Primary Capital Pressure: Revenue-producing vehicles need capital before new routes/jobs produce cash.
Typical Trades: Trucking; last-mile delivery; towing; HVAC; plumbing; moving; septic; landscaping; field services
What It Covers: Tractors, trailers, service vans, flatbeds, wreckers, box trucks, vehicle repair/replacement.
Best Signals: Fleet photos; driver hiring; new routes/contracts; service area expansion; vehicle repair posts; multi-truck operation.
Not A Fit When: Solo light-asset business with no visible vehicle dependency.
Outreach Angle: If trucks are the bottleneck, fleet funding can help keep jobs moving without draining operating cash.
Example CTA: Worth looking at a fleet option or two?
Compliance Notes: Avoid claiming vehicle/title collateral is sufficient without full qualification.

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: General cash need tied to growth, payroll, repairs, materials, insurance, fuel, staffing, or short-term operating pressure.
Primary Capital Pressure: Flexible cash is needed to bridge timing gaps or support growth.
What It Covers: Payroll, materials, repairs, rent, insurance, marketing, staffing, general business cash needs.
Best Signals: Hiring; expansion; seasonal ramp; busy reviews; increased service volume; no single asset need.
Not A Fit When: Specific receivables or equipment product is a better, cleaner fit.
Outreach Angle: Flexible capital can cover the messy middle between growth and collected revenue.
Compliance Notes: Do not describe as best/cheapest; frame as one possible fit.

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: Recurring or unpredictable cash gaps where the operator needs draw-as-needed flexibility.
Primary Capital Pressure: Cash needs fluctuate by job, season, route, or receivable timing.
Typical Trades: HVAC; plumbing; roofing; landscaping; auto repair; cleaning; contractors; service businesses with recurring deposits
What It Covers: Revolving access for materials, payroll, repairs, inventory, short cash gaps.
Best Signals: Recurring contracts; seasonal variability; multiple small cash gaps; established business activity.
Not A Fit When: Business only needs one specific asset or has severe red flags.
Outreach Angle: A line gives you a cash safety valve without borrowing more than the job actually needs.
Example CTA: Want me to compare a line versus a lump-sum option?
Compliance Notes: Do not imply revolving approval or credit limit before review.

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: Urgent cash need, strong recent revenue/deposits, fast-moving opportunity, or weaker traditional credit profile.
Primary Capital Pressure: Speed matters more than lowest cost.
Typical Trades: Auto repair; HVAC; plumbing; restoration; high-ticket service shops; local operators with steady card/bank deposits
What It Covers: Fast operating cash repaid from future revenue or frequent payments.
Best Signals: Strong service demand; daily/weekly deposits; urgent repair/payroll/materials need; no time for bank paperwork.
Not A Fit When: Existing debt stack is heavy; revenue is weak/uneven; lower-cost financing is available and timing allows.
Outreach Angle: If the opportunity or emergency cannot wait on a bank, revenue-based capital may bridge the gap.
Example CTA: Want me to check whether fast working capital even makes sense here?
Compliance Notes: Avoid softening cost/risk; do not promise same-day funding or approval.

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: Business waits on commercial clients, insurance claims, project draws, retainage, or net-30/60/90 invoices.
Primary Capital Pressure: Work is completed or underway, but cash collection lags payroll and suppliers.
Typical Trades: Restoration; commercial cleaning; general contractors; electrical; plumbing; trucking; government/commercial contractors
What It Covers: Advance/bridge against invoices, insurance receivables, project draws, or slow customer payments.
Best Signals: Insurance claim support; commercial contracts; net terms; government work; project draw language; receivable complaints.
Not A Fit When: No invoice/receivable basis exists or customers are mostly consumer COD.
Outreach Angle: The work may be done, but bills do not wait for customers, carriers, or insurance companies to pay.
Example CTA: Want me to show a receivables bridge example?
Compliance Notes: Do not claim invoices are eligible until verified.

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: Carrier waits on broker/shipper payments while fuel, repairs, insurance, and driver pay hit first.
Primary Capital Pressure: Freight revenue is delayed by broker/shipper payment cycles.
Typical Trades: Trucking; logistics; last-mile delivery; freight carriers
What It Covers: Advances against freight invoices, often on a recurring basis.
Best Signals: Freight routes; CDL hiring; broker/shipper pay; fleet growth; fuel pressure; dispatch growth.
Not A Fit When: Not a freight carrier or invoices are not factorable.
Outreach Angle: Get paid on loads sooner instead of letting brokers dictate your cash calendar.
Example CTA: Want to compare factoring versus a working-capital bridge?
Compliance Notes: Do not mention factoring terms, advance rates, or fees unless verified.

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: Large material order, supply restock, unit inventory, parts inventory, or project materials needed before payment arrives.
Primary Capital Pressure: Materials or inventory must be purchased upfront.
Typical Trades: Roofing; concrete; paving; electrical; plumbing; HVAC; auto repair; general contracting
What It Covers: Materials, supplies, parts, HVAC units, roofing materials, concrete/asphalt inputs, project stock.
Best Signals: Large jobs; backlog; supplier costs; seasonal stocking; parts shortage; commercial contract; insurance job.
Not A Fit When: Need is primarily labor/fleet/marketing rather than materials.
Outreach Angle: Materials often hit before the job pays. A short bridge can protect cash flow.
Example CTA: Want me to send a quick materials-funding example?
Compliance Notes: Avoid implying supplier purchases are automatically fundable.

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: Hiring crews, techs, drivers, dispatchers, or seasonal workers before revenue from added capacity is collected.
Typical Trades: Roofing; landscaping; restoration; HVAC; trucking; cleaning; moving; contractors
What It Covers: Short-term payroll cycles, onboarding labor, seasonal labor ramp, crew expansion.
Best Signals: Job posts; hiring spree; busy season; backlog; new contracts; service expansion.
Not A Fit When: No growth/demand signal and payroll issue looks like distress rather than ramp.
Outreach Angle: Hiring is a good sign, but payroll can squeeze cash before new revenue catches up.
Example CTA: Want to see what a payroll bridge might look like?
Compliance Notes: Do not imply the business cannot make payroll; phrase as timing/ramp pressure.

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: Large contract, commercial job, mobilization cost, draw schedule, retainage, subcontractor payments, or upfront project costs.
Primary Capital Pressure: Big job requires cash before draws or final payment.
Typical Trades: General contracting; electrical; concrete; paving; excavation; commercial trades; restoration
What It Covers: Mobilization, subs, materials, labor, equipment rental, project-specific cash needs.
Best Signals: Commercial contract; municipal project; project backlog; new job announcement; draw/retainage language.
Not A Fit When: No specific project or backlog exists.
Outreach Angle: A big job can be a blessing and a cash-flow ambush at the same time.
Example CTA: Want to map the project cash gap?
Compliance Notes: Do not represent as construction lending approval; needs project review.

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: Established stable operator seeking larger, lower-cost, longer-term capital for expansion, acquisition, real estate, or major refinancing.
Primary Capital Pressure: Long-term growth need where lower cost matters more than speed.
Typical Trades: Established HVAC; plumbing; auto repair; multi-location home services; stable contractors
What It Covers: Expansion, acquisition, owner-occupied real estate, refinance, large working capital needs.
Best Signals: Years in business; strong footprint; multi-location; stable reviews; acquisition/real estate plans.
Not A Fit When: Urgent same-week cash need or very thin/new business.
Outreach Angle: If the move is strategic and you can wait on paperwork, SBA-style capital may be worth comparing.
Example CTA: Want me to sanity-check whether SBA is realistic or too slow?
Compliance Notes: Do not state SBA eligibility; lender/guaranty requirements vary.

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: New location, service-area expansion, new service line, franchise growth, acquisition, or added crews/vehicles.
Primary Capital Pressure: Growth costs hit before the new revenue stabilizes.
Typical Trades: HVAC; roofing; pest control; home services; franchises; auto repair; regional service operators
What It Covers: Buildout, hiring, vehicles, marketing, inventory, training, acquisition support.
Best Signals: New branch/service area; multiple job posts; franchise/acquisition language; marketing push.
Not A Fit When: No clear growth path or expansion is speculative.
Outreach Angle: Expansion usually needs cash before the new area starts paying for itself.
Compliance Notes: Avoid assuming growth is profitable; frame as planning support.

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: Rebrand, new website, paid ad push, local SEO campaign, new service-area launch, or seasonal lead capture.
Primary Capital Pressure: Lead generation spend occurs before new jobs pay.
Typical Trades: Roofing; HVAC; plumbing; pest control; auto repair; home services; seasonal trades
What It Covers: Ads, SEO, website, direct mail, canvassing, branding, lead generation.
Best Signals: New website; active ads; rebrand; new market; seasonal campaign; hiring sales reps.
Not A Fit When: No marketing/growth signal or business has severe reputation issues.
Outreach Angle: A marketing push can work, but only if cash survives the ramp.
Example CTA: Want to see whether funding the campaign makes sense?

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: Fuel, insurance, repairs, tolls, route growth, or variable operating costs strain cash between receipts.
Primary Capital Pressure: High variable costs hit before routes/jobs pay.
Typical Trades: Trucking; towing; last-mile delivery; landscaping; excavation; moving; fleet-heavy trades
What It Covers: Fuel, insurance, repairs, temporary operating cushion.
Best Signals: Fleet dependence; route growth; driver hiring; fuel-heavy work; seasonal travel; equipment operation.
Outreach Angle: Fuel can quietly drain margin even when routes or jobs look profitable.
Example CTA: Want to look at a short buffer instead of parking trucks?
Compliance Notes: Do not imply distress; position as timing/operating cushion.

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: Pre-season ramp, off-season carry, inventory/staffing prep, repairs, or seasonal revenue gap.
Typical Trades: Landscaping; lawn care; snow removal; pool service; HVAC; pest control; roofing
What It Covers: Equipment prep, payroll, materials/supplies, marketing, slow-season bridge.
Best Signals: Seasonal trade; pre-season hiring; equipment prep; booked contracts; peak demand window.
Not A Fit When: No seasonal pattern or severe red flags.
Outreach Angle: The season pays later, but prep costs hit now.
Example CTA: Want to map the season’s cash gap before it hits?
Compliance Notes: Do not promise seasonal revenue or repayment fit.

Best-Fit Situation / Trigger: Multiple advances/loans, stacked payments, heavy daily/weekly debits, or cash-flow stress from existing obligations.
Primary Capital Pressure: Debt service crowds out payroll, materials, or operations.
Typical Trades: Any local service business with existing debt stack
What It Covers: Review of refinancing, consolidation, payoff, or restructuring options when available.
Best Signals: User-provided debt info; applicant says payments are too high; bank statements show multiple debits.
Not A Fit When: No debt details are provided; avoid inferring debt distress from public data.
Outreach Angle: If payments are crowding payroll or materials, it may be worth reviewing options before it gets uglier.
Example CTA: Want me to review the debt stack before you add anything new?
Compliance Notes: Do not diagnose distress from public data; use only when provided by user/client.
